text: The surgeon encounters bleeding during a left-sided pulmonary lobectomy. The medial robotic arm, equipped with a Cartier Arm One, applies pressure with a sponge to the bleeding site, focusing on the pulmonary artery and vein. The camera is repositioned through the assistant port for continuous visualization of the bleeder. Instruments are removed to prepare for conversion to a posterolateral thoracotomy, allowing manual control of the bleeding. The robotic arm holding pressure is withdrawn, and the robot is moved out slowly and controlled.

During the left lower lobectomy, the surgeon encounters a positive hilar node and performs a dissection with caution. A spondylole is positioned above the dissection site to hold pressure in case of bleeding. As bleeding occurs, the surgeon uses the spondylole to apply pressure to the pulmonary artery, a low-pressure system, while the camera captures the spurt of blood. The camera is then removed for cleaning.

The surgeon uses a suction device to remove blood from the surgical field while maintaining pressure on the bleeder to control the bleeding from the pulmonary artery. The robotic arms remain in place as the surgeon steps away from the console to assess the situation and communicate with the surgical team. After holding pressure for three minutes, the surgeon evaluates the bleeding control, considering whether to continue the procedure or convert to an open thoracotomy.

The surgeon discusses the approach to controlling bleeding during a pulmonary lobectomy, emphasizing the use of the medial arm to hold pressure on the bleeder. For a left-sided resection, the medial arm is identified as Arm One, while for a right-sided resection, it would be Arm Four. The surgeon plans to convert to a posterolateral thoracotomy, removing the lateral arms, including the camera port, to safely manage the bleeding.

The surgeon holds pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm, while the camera is repositioned from arm two to the assistant port for continuous visualization of the sponge stick on the bleeder. The lateral three robotic ports are removed to facilitate the conversion to a posterolateral thoracotomy.

After completing the thoracotomy, the surgeon manually controls the bleeding through the thoracotomy incision. Arm one of the robotic system is removed, allowing the entire robot to be moved out of the surgical field for a safe conversion.

The surgeon attempts an anteromedial basilar segmentectomy on the left lower lobe, working on small pulmonary artery branches in a tight space. A sponge roll is visible in the chest, ready to hold pressure in case of bleeding. As the robotic stapler is fired across the space, bleeding occurs. The surgeon holds pressure with the sponge roll without grabbing the bleeder, maintaining control in the low-pressure system. The assistant uses a suction device to manage the bleeding, while preparations for thoracotomy conversion are underway.

The surgeon maintains pressure on the bleeder using the medial robotic arm, while the assistant removes the camera from arm two and places it through the assistant port for continuous visualization. The lateral robotic ports are removed, and the surgeon scrubs in preparation for a posterolateral thoracotomy through the fifth intercostal space. The robotic arm holding pressure is slid out, and the robot is moved aside to allow manual control of the bleeder.
